issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-paris-city-of-light-enlightenment asks 'Why was Paris originally given the nickname City of Light?' with the same answer and near-identical explanation. Two identical facts in the same batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-paris-city-of-light. Keep geography-paris-city-of-light-enlightenment which has the same content.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-kolkata-british-capital asks nearly the identical question ('Which Indian city was the second largest in the British Empire?' vs 'Which Indian city served as the second-largest city in the entire British Empire?'). Two versions of the same fact in one batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-kolkata-second-british-empire. The geo_kolkata_british_capital version is slightly more informative (it specifies 1911 as the year the capital moved to Delhi).

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geo_kolkata_only_chinatown asks the near-identical question ('Which Indian city contains the country's only Chinatown?' vs Kolkata's Tiretti Bazaar). Two versions of the same Chinatown fact in one batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-kolkata-only-chinatown. The geo_kolkata_only_chinatown version is more specific and interesting (it names Tiretti Bazaar and provides richer context about Hakka Chinese immigrants).

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-andorra-co-princes asks 'Which country is jointly ruled by a bishop and a foreign president?' — essentially the same question as geography-andorra-two-co-princes ('Which European country is jointly ruled by a French president and a Spanish bishop?'). Same fact, two questions.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-andorra-co-princes. The geography-andorra-two-co-princes version is more informative (specifies French president and Spanish bishop).

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-lithuania-first-soviet-independence asks the identical question ('Which was the first Soviet republic to declare independence from the USSR?'). Two copies of the same fact in one batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-lithuania-first-soviet-break. Both are nearly identical; keep geography-lithuania-first-soviet-independence which has a slightly richer explanation.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-croatia-independence-879 asks the same question about Croatia's 879 CE recognition from a different angle ('In what century was Croatia first recognized internationally as independent?'). Two facts about the same 879 CE event in one batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-croatia-recognized-879 (the numerical year version). Keep geography-croatia-independence-879 which asks for the century — a more interesting framing that requires understanding rather than memorizing a specific number.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-macau-casino-vs-las-vegas asks the identical question ('How does Macau's gambling industry compare to Las Vegas in size?' / 'How does Macau's gambling industry compare in size to Las Vegas?'). Same fact, same answer, two questions.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-macau-densest-gambling. Keep geography-macau-casino-vs-las-vegas which has a cleaner ID and more detailed explanation.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-sydney-first-fleet-1788 asks the same foundational fact ('What was Sydney's original purpose when founded by the British in 1788?' / 'What was the unusual founding population of Sydney in 1788?'). Same event, answers 'Penal colony' vs 'Convicts' — effectively identical.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-sydney-first-fleet-1788. Keep geography-sydney-penal-colony which has a cleaner answer ('Penal colony') that better fits a multiple choice game.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-magellan-died-circumnavigation asks the identical question ('Who led the first circumnavigation of Earth but died before it finished?' / 'Which explorer led the first circumnavigation of Earth but died before it finished?'). Exact same fact, same answer.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-magellan-circumnavigation. Keep geography-magellan-died-circumnavigation which has a more precise explanation (mentions 18 survivors from 270 crew) and is correctly categorized under landforms_water.

issues.description: States 'Over {15} million' for Kolkata's metropolitan population, but the sibling fact geo_kolkata_metro_population states '{14} million'. The two figures are inconsistent within the same batch. Wikipedia figures for Kolkata metro vary by source (14–15 million), but presenting both as definitive answers in the same game creates a contradiction.
issues.suggested_fix: Standardize to 'About {15} million' across both facts (the higher estimate is more current), or retire one of the two Kolkata metro population facts.

issues.description: States '{14} million' for Kolkata's metropolitan area population, conflicting with geography-kolkata-metro-population which states 'Over {15} million'. Both appear in the same batch. See geography-kolkata-metro-population note above.
issues.suggested_fix: Standardize to 'About {15} million' or retire this duplicate in favor of geography-kolkata-metro-population.

issues.description: Duplicate topic — geography-himalayas-100-peaks-over-7200m asks the nearly identical question ('How many Himalayan peaks exceed 7,200 meters above sea level?' vs '... metres above sea level?'). These are the same question with only metre/meter spelling variation.
issues.suggested_fix: Retire geography-himalayas-100-peaks-7200m. Keep geography-himalayas-100-peaks-over-7200m which has a slightly better explanation connecting to the range's length.

issues.description: The explanation goes off on a tangent: 'making France (whose president is a co-prince of Andorra) the only republic that is also a monarchy.' This non-sequitur about France and Andorra has nothing to do with the question about how the Pope is chosen. It is confusing and sounds like a Wikidata data dump artifact.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the final sentence about France and Andorra from the explanation. Keep the explanation focused: 'Vatican City is governed by the Holy See and the Pope holds absolute executive, legislative, and judicial authority. Unlike hereditary monarchies, the Pope is elected by the College of Cardinals in a conclave held inside the Sistine Chapel.'

issues.description: The question asks 'Which European country was first to grant universal suffrage, in 1906?' — but New Zealand granted women the vote in 1893 (though not the right to stand for election). Finland's actual distinction is being first to grant *full* political rights (vote AND stand for office). 'Universal suffrage' is ambiguous and could lead knowledgeable players to dispute the answer.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase to: 'Which European country was first to grant women full political rights — both the vote and the right to stand for election — in 1906?' This matches the more precise framing in the sibling fact geo_finland_universal_suffrage.

issues.description: The correct_answer is '{180000}' with no formatting (no comma separator, no units). In a game context this renders as '180000' which is harder to read than '180,000'. The sibling fact geography-finland-lakes correctly uses 'Over {188,000} lakes' with comma formatting and units.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'About {180,000} lakes' for readability consistency, or use 'About {188,000} lakes' to match the more precise figure cited in the sibling fact.

issues.description: States Germany has 55 UNESCO World Heritage Sites and 'ranks third globally... behind only Italy and China.' As of 2024–2026, China has 57 sites, Italy has 59, and Germany has 54 (figures are updated annually). The exact count of 55 may be slightly off and the ranking claim needs verification. Minor but worth checking against the current UNESCO list.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase as 'Over {50}' to make it more durable against annual UNESCO list updates, or verify the current count and update accordingly.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which country' but the correct answer is 'England', which is a constituent country of the UK, not a sovereign country. The question and answer are inconsistent — the Industrial Revolution began in England specifically, not in the UK as a whole, but calling England a 'country' in isolation is technically accurate yet potentially confusing. More importantly, the distractor 'Scotland' is problematic: Scotland is also part of Great Britain, so answering 'England' vs 'Scotland' muddies the 'country' framing.
issues.suggested_fix: Change question to 'Which part of the British Isles became the world's first industrialised nation in the 18th century?' or change correct_answer to 'Great Britain' / 'United Kingdom' to match the 'country' framing.

issues.description: Finland and Sweden are listed as distractors implying they are NATO members, which is correct as of 2023–2024 (Finland joined April 2023, Sweden March 2024). However, Austria, Switzerland, Malta, and Cyprus are all genuinely non-NATO, making them poor distractors — a player could reasonably second-guess Ireland by selecting one of those instead. The question asks for 'which Western European nation stays out of NATO due to military neutrality,' but Austria and Switzerland also have long-standing formal neutrality policies and also stay out of NATO, making the correct answer ambiguous.
issues.suggested_fix: The question needs rewording to make Ireland uniquely correct. Suggested: 'Which island nation in Western Europe is not a NATO member due to a policy of military neutrality?' — or replace Austria/Switzerland from the distractor pool since they are also correct answers to the question as written. This is a real ambiguity issue.

issues.description: The claim that Mount Etna is 'Europe's tallest active volcano' is accurate, but the question asks which volcano is 'Europe's tallest' without the word 'active.' Mount Elbrus in Russia (5,642 m) is taller than Etna (~3,350 m) and is technically a dormant (not extinct) stratovolcano. The explanation only mentions 'tallest active volcano in Europe' (correct) but the question omits 'active,' making it potentially misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Add 'active' to the question: 'Which active volcano is both Sicily's highest point and Europe's tallest active volcano?'

issues.description: The distractor 'Irish monks' is actually a competing historically plausible answer — Saint Brendan's voyage (c. 512–530 CE) is a serious historical claim that some scholars believe reached North America before the Vikings. While mainstream consensus favors the Norse, including 'Irish monks' as a distractor without acknowledging the debate makes the question appear to present settled fact where there is genuine academic discussion. Additionally, 'Basque fishermen' reaching Newfoundland before Columbus is another well-documented historical claim.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Irish monks' and 'Basque fishermen' with less plausible distractors (e.g., 'Ottoman merchants,' 'Byzantine navigators'). Alternatively, tighten the question to 'Which group left confirmed archaeological evidence of reaching North America before Columbus?' to make the Norse answer unambiguous.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which Asian city-state has the world's highest PPP-adjusted GDP per capita?' but the distractor list includes 'Macau' — which by many measures (including IMF PPP data) has a higher GDP per capita than Singapore. Macau's GDP per capita has regularly exceeded Singapore's in PPP terms, particularly during casino boom years. Including Macau as a 'wrong' distractor is factually questionable.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove 'Macau' from the distractor list and replace with a less competitive option (e.g., 'South Korea' or 'Taiwan'). Alternatively, add a year qualifier to the question to anchor it to a specific dataset.

issues.description: The question asks 'What made colonial Manila a global trade powerhouse?' — a 'why/what' question expecting a reason or explanation. The correct answer 'Trans-Pacific trade hub' is a noun phrase label rather than a reason. It reads awkwardly as a standalone answer to 'what made it...'. Compare to: 'It became the western hub of trans-Pacific trade.'
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase the correct answer to 'It was the hub of trans-Pacific trade' or rephrase the question to 'What role did colonial Manila play in global trade?' so the noun-phrase answer fits naturally.

issues.description: The explanation states 'Venice was at war with Genoa when Marco Polo returned home in 1299' and 'He was released in 1299.' The Battle of Curzola (where Polo was captured) was in 1298, and most sources place his release around 1299. However, the explanation says he 'returned home in 1299' AND 'was released in 1299' — this implies he was captured immediately on return, which is slightly misleading. He returned from Asia around 1295, joined the war around 1296–1298, was captured at Curzola in 1298, and released in 1299. The 1299 return date in the explanation is inaccurate.
issues.suggested_fix: Correct the explanation: 'Marco Polo returned to Venice around 1295. He joined the Venetian war effort against Genoa, was captured at the Battle of Curzola in 1298, and while imprisoned dictated his travels to cellmate Rustichello da Pisa. He was released in 1299.'

issues.description: The distractor list contains 'Vanuatu' twice (appears at positions 4 and 8 in the array). Duplicate distractors are a data quality error.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace the second 'Vanuatu' with a unique distractor such as 'Cook Islands' or 'Niue.'

issues.description: 'Indian Ocean' appears twice in the distractor list (positions 3 and 8). Duplicate distractors are a data quality error.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace one 'Indian Ocean' with a unique option such as 'Arctic Ocean' or 'Antarctic Ocean.'

issues.description: 'Bund' appears twice in the distractor list (positions 4 and 8). The Bund is actually a waterfront area, not a district in the same administrative sense as Pudong — but its appearance twice is a data error regardless.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the duplicate 'Bund' entry and replace the second one with a distinct Shanghai district such as 'Changning' or 'Putuo.'

issues.description: The explanation acknowledges tango 'originated in the working-class port neighborhoods of Buenos Aires AND Montevideo' — yet the question asks 'In which city did the tango dance originate?' and the correct answer is 'Buenos Aires' only. If the explanation itself says Montevideo is a co-origin city, the question has an ambiguous correct answer. Uruguay (Montevideo) contests Argentina's sole claim to tango's origins, and UNESCO recognized it as shared heritage of both countries.
issues.suggested_fix: Either change the question to 'In which Argentine city is tango most associated with?' (making Buenos Aires unambiguously correct), or change the correct answer to 'Buenos Aires and Montevideo' and rework as a multiple-select or narrative fact. At minimum, remove Montevideo from the explanation's origin claim or acknowledge the shared origin properly.

issues.description: This fact is categorized under category_l2 'europe' but Palestine is in Asia (Middle East). This is a miscategorization — the fact should be under 'asia_oceania' to match its sister fact 'geography-palestine-oslo-accords-1993' and 'geo-palestine-crossroads-of-empires.'
issues.suggested_fix: Change category_l2 from 'europe' to 'asia_oceania.'

issues.description: The question asks 'How often does Latvia's Song and Dance Festival take place?' and the answer is 'Every 5 years.' However, the Song Festival (Dziesmu svētki) and Dance Festival (Deju svētki) are actually separate events held every 5 years each, but offset so that one occurs every few years. More critically, multiple sources (including the official festival website) indicate the combined festival is held every 4–5 years, not on a fixed 5-year cycle. The explanation does not address the frequency claim at all, only describing its history.
issues.suggested_fix: Verify the current official cycle. If truly every 5 years, update the explanation to confirm this. If the cycle is irregular or 4 years, correct the answer. At minimum, the explanation should directly state the frequency to support the answer.

issues.description: The correct answer 'None (non-voting only)' is slightly self-contradictory. 'None' followed by '(non-voting only)' suggests there IS representation — just non-voting. The parenthetical undermines the main claim. The explanation correctly clarifies that D.C. has a non-voting delegate in the House, so the answer should be precise.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'Non-voting delegate only — no voting representation' or 'A non-voting House delegate (no senators)' for clarity and internal consistency.

issues.description: The claim that the Amazon rainforest produces 'about 20% of the world's oxygen' is a widely repeated but scientifically contested figure. The Amazon produces roughly 20% of global oxygen through photosynthesis but also consumes a nearly equal amount through respiration and decomposition — making its net oxygen contribution close to zero or even slightly negative. The '20% of oxygen' figure is considered a popular myth by many ecologists. The Amazon's actual climate importance is as a massive carbon sink, not an oxygen source.
issues.suggested_fix: Change the question and answer to focus on the Amazon's role as a carbon sink or its share of the world's tropical forest cover (about 40%), which are factually accurate and educationally sound. Example: 'What fraction of the world's tropical rainforest does the Amazon basin contain?' → 'About 40%'

issues.description: The question claims Colombia is 'unique among South American countries' for bordering two oceans. However, the Caribbean Sea is technically not an ocean — it is a marginal sea of the Atlantic Ocean. If the Caribbean counts as 'the Atlantic,' then Ecuador, Peru, and Chile also border the Pacific, and Colombia's uniqueness claim breaks down unless specifically framed as Caribbean + Pacific. The explanation says 'Pacific Ocean (to the west) and the Caribbean Sea (to the north)' — calling the Caribbean an ocean when the question says 'two oceans' is imprecise.
issues.suggested_fix: Revise to: 'What makes Colombia unique among South American nations is that it has coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea.' Keep the question but clarify in the explanation that 'Caribbean Sea' is sometimes informally called an ocean in this context, or rephrase the question to 'Which South American country has coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea?'

issues.description: Columbus's first voyage departed from Palos de la Frontera (near Huelva), not Seville. The question says 'his voyages' (plural), but the explanation itself clarifies he only organized his 3rd and 4th voyages from Seville. A player who knows the first voyage left from Palos would correctly contest the answer.
issues.suggested_fix: Narrow the question: 'From which Spanish city did Columbus organize his later voyages to the Americas?' or 'Which Spanish city was the administrative capital of Spain's colonial trade empire?' Both have Seville as the correct answer but avoid the inaccuracy about all voyages.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which German city is also an entire federal state unto itself?' implying uniqueness, but the explanation correctly notes that Berlin and Bremen share this exact status. A player who knows Berlin is also a city-state would find the question misleading or unanswerable.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase to remove the uniqueness implication: 'Which of the following German cities is simultaneously one of the country's 16 federal states?' Or add 'along with Berlin and Bremen' to the question stem.

issues.description: The answer 'Four' (Spanish, English, Portuguese, French) excludes Dutch, which is the official language of Suriname and the Dutch Caribbean territories. The qualifier 'major' is doing a lot of work here and is subjective. A player who knows about Suriname has a legitimate case for Five.
issues.suggested_fix: Add precision: 'How many European colonial languages are spoken as official languages by the most populous nations of the Americas?' or acknowledge Dutch in the explanation and justify its exclusion explicitly.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of geography-naples-pizza later in the batch. Both ask about Naples as the birthplace of pizza (one asks generally about pizza, one about pizza Margherita in 1889), both give Naples as the answer, and both cite Raffaele Esposito. Players will see both questions and the second will feel like a repeat.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one, or differentiate clearly: keep one for the general origin of pizza in Naples and pivot the other to a distinct angle such as 'What protected EU status does Neapolitan pizza hold?' or 'Who made pizza Margherita for Queen Margherita in 1889?' (answer: Raffaele Esposito).

issues.description: Near-duplicate of geography-naples-pizza-origin — both teach the same Naples/pizza/Raffaele Esposito fact with the same correct answer. See note on geography-naples-pizza-origin above.
issues.suggested_fix: Retain only one Naples-pizza fact, or pivot this one to a distinct question angle (e.g., 'What Italian city's pizza style is protected by EU law under DOP status?' answer: Naples).

issues.description: Near-duplicate of geography-cologne-cathedral later in the batch. Both ask how long Cologne Cathedral took to build and both use the 1248–1880 dates. The first gives 'Over {600} years' and the second gives '{632} years' — players will see both questions and the second gives away the answer to the first.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one of the two. If both are kept, differentiate: one could ask 'In what year was Cologne Cathedral finally completed after a 632-year build?' (answer: {1880}) while the other keeps the duration angle.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of geography-cologne-cathedral-construction — both cover the same 1248–1880 construction timeline. See note above.
issues.suggested_fix: Retain one; pivot the other to a distinct angle such as 'Which German cathedral was briefly the world's tallest structure upon its completion in 1880?' (answer: Cologne Cathedral).

issues.description: The distractor 'George Castriot' is Skanderbeg's actual birth name, as stated in the explanation: 'born George Castriot.' This is a factually correct answer being offered as a wrong answer. Any player who knows this will be penalized for selecting what is, in fact, the right person.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'George Castriot' with a different distractor — e.g., 'Ali Pasha of Ioannina,' 'Gjergj Fishta,' or another Balkan historical figure. The correct answer and the distractor must not refer to the same person.

issues.description: The question 'Whose father united most of Greece before he conquered the ancient world?' is structurally ambiguous — 'he' could refer to the father (Philip II, who did the uniting) or the son (Alexander, who did the conquering). A player reading quickly is likely to answer 'Philip II' since he is the explicit subject of the first clause.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase to remove ambiguity: 'Philip II of Macedon unified most of Greece so that his son could conquer the ancient world. Who was that son?' (answer: Alexander the Great). Or: 'Which conqueror's father, Philip II, first unified the Greek city-states?'

issues.description: Kazakhstan is listed as a distractor for 'largest country in Europe after Russia.' Kazakhstan straddles Europe and Asia — its western portion is in Europe — and if counted as European it is actually larger than Ukraine, making it a technically correct answer rather than a plausible wrong one. This creates a factual trap.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Kazakhstan' with a clearly European large country such as 'Spain' or 'Sweden,' both of which are large but smaller than Ukraine, making them genuinely plausible-but-wrong distractors.

issues.description: The explanation conflates two distinct etymologies. 'Deutsch' (from Deutschland) derives from Proto-Germanic *þiudisk meaning 'of the people' or 'vernacular' — not from 'muteness.' The 'mute/foreign' etymology (meaning people who speak unintelligibly) applies to Slavic words for Germans, notably Russian Nemtsy (Немцы). The explanation's claim that 'Deutsch' originally meant 'mutes' is incorrect; that meaning belongs to a completely separate linguistic tradition.
issues.suggested_fix: Either correct the explanation: 'Deutsch comes from *þiudisk meaning the vernacular/people's language, while Slavic languages called Germans Nemtsy (mute ones) — and the answer/question should reflect which etymology is actually being asked about.' Or narrow the question to 'What does the Russian/Slavic word for Germans (Nemtsy) literally mean?' with answer 'Mute people' or 'Those who cannot speak.'

issues.description: The answer 'At least {4}' is inconsistent with the explanation, which lists seven distinct political entities: medieval Poland, Austria-Hungary, briefly Germany (1918), interwar Poland, Nazi Germany, Soviet Union, and independent Ukraine. The answer of 4 significantly understates the number described in the explanation, creating confusion.
issues.suggested_fix: Change the answer to 'At least {6}' to match the explanation, or simplify the explanation to list exactly four major powers (Poland, Austria-Hungary, Nazi Germany, Soviet Union) without the interim overlaps, so the number and explanation agree.

issues.description: The correct_answer uses brace notation inconsistently: 'Between {20,000} and 16,000 years ago' — the first number is in braces but the second is not. For a numerical range answer both should be formatted consistently. Additionally, recent evidence (Chiquihuite Cave) suggests arrival as early as 30,000 years ago, making the upper bound potentially outdated.
issues.suggested_fix: Fix brace formatting to 'Between {20,000} and {16,000} years ago.' Optionally add a note in the explanation that some sites suggest earlier arrival dates, making this an area of active research.

issues.description: The question asks for 'the largest English-speaking country in Latin America and the Caribbean' and gives Jamaica as the answer based on being the 'largest English-speaking country.' However, Belize (~22,966 km²) is larger by area than Jamaica (~10,990 km²) and is also English-speaking and in the region. Jamaica is the most populous English-speaking Caribbean island nation, but not the largest by land area.
issues.suggested_fix: Change 'largest' to 'most populous': 'What is the most populous English-speaking country in Latin America and the Caribbean?' — Jamaica (~3 million) clearly beats Belize (~400,000) on this metric. Or specify 'largest English-speaking island nation in the Caribbean' to exclude mainland Belize.

issues.description: The fact ID is 'geography-valencia-silk-capital' but the question is about Valencia's role as provisional capital during the Spanish Civil War — nothing about silk. The explanation then mentions both silk (18th century) and the Civil War seat of government. Two unrelated facts about Valencia have been blended into one entry under a misleading ID.
issues.suggested_fix: Split into two facts or pick one angle. If keeping the Civil War question, rename the ID to 'geography-valencia-civil-war-capital' and remove the silk reference from the explanation (or move it to a separate fact). The current ID-question mismatch is confusing for content management.

issues.description: The fact ID is 'geo-new-caledonia-nickel-reserves' but the question asks 'How many provinces is New Caledonia divided into?' — nothing about nickel. The question, answer ({3} provinces), and explanation are all about provincial divisions. The ID suggests this fact was originally planned as a nickel reserves question and the content was replaced without updating the ID.
issues.suggested_fix: Rename the ID to 'geo-new-caledonia-provinces' to match the actual content. If a nickel reserves fact was intended, create it separately (New Caledonia holds ~25% of the world's known nickel reserves — a genuinely interesting fact).

issues.description: Several distractors describe non-existent national attributes ('Only with no flag,' 'Only Creole-speaking') rather than plausible wrong answers about actual South American countries. These are not plausible wrong answers a player would seriously consider — they are clearly absurd. 'Only French territory' is also odd since French Guiana is a real entity (though not a country).
issues.suggested_fix: Replace weak distractors with country-based answers that name actual South American countries and their linguistic distinctions: e.g., 'Only Spanish-speaking (Argentina/Colombia/etc.),' 'Only Portuguese-speaking (Brazil),' 'Only Dutch-speaking (Suriname),' 'Only French-speaking (French Guiana).' These are genuinely plausible wrong answers.

issues.description: This fact states the Canal shortened travel by ~12,875 km (New York to San Francisco route). A separate fact in the same batch (geography-panama-canal) states the Canal saves 'up to 15,000 km.' Both figures are technically valid for different route comparisons, but a player seeing both facts will encounter two different numbers for apparently the same question and find them contradictory.
issues.suggested_fix: Ensure each Canal fact specifies the exact route being measured: 'New York to San Francisco via Panama saves ~12,875 km versus the Cape Horn route.' The other fact should specify it refers to the maximum savings on the most extreme routes. Adding route specificity to both prevents apparent contradiction.

issues.description: Guam is NOT the westernmost point of the United States. The westernmost point is Orote Point on Guam, but more accurately the westernmost US territory by longitude is actually Wake Island or parts of the Northern Mariana Islands depending on methodology. More critically, the explanation itself says 'westernmost point and territory as measured from the geographic centre of the US' — that is a non-standard and confusing qualifier. The standard geographic answer for westernmost US territory is the Northern Mariana Islands (specifically Farallon de Pajaros), not Guam.
issues.suggested_fix: Either change the correct answer to 'Northern Mariana Islands' (which is further west than Guam), or rewrite the question to ask specifically about Guam's geographic distinction without claiming it's westernmost US territory, which is factually disputed. If the intent is to say Guam is the westernmost US territory in Micronesia, clarify that.

issues.description: 'North Rhine-Westphalia' appears twice in the distractor list (positions 1 and 8). Duplicate distractors are a data quality error.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace the duplicate 'North Rhine-Westphalia' with a different German state, e.g. 'Thuringia' or 'Mecklenburg-Vorpommern'.

issues.description: 'Black Forest' is in the distractor list as if it were a mountain name comparable to the others. The Black Forest (Schwarzwald) is a forested mountain range/region, not a single named peak. It's categorically different from the named summits in the list and would confuse players.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Black Forest' with a specific peak name, e.g. 'Zugspitzplatt' or 'Alpspitze' or 'Nebelhorn'.

issues.description: 'Wolfsburg' appears as a distractor in a question about German states ranked by GDP. Wolfsburg is a city, not a German state (Bundesland). It is categorically wrong as a distractor and would be immediately recognizable as absurd.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Wolfsburg' with a real German state name, e.g. 'Saxony-Anhalt' or 'Saarland'.

issues.description: 'Reims' appears as a distractor in a question about Stuttgart's rank among German cities. Reims is a French city, not a German city rank. It is categorically impossible as an answer and breaks immersion.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Reims' with an ordinal like 'Ninth' or 'Eleventh'.

issues.description: 'Florence' appears as a distractor in a question specifically about French cities with the most listed historic monuments. Florence is an Italian city and cannot be a valid answer to a question about France's ranking. It breaks the category logic entirely.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Florence' with a French city, e.g. 'Reims', 'Avignon', or 'Montpellier'.

issues.description: 'Haiti' appears as a distractor in a question about which Italian city is headquarters to Fiat, Lancia, and Alfa Romeo. Haiti is a Caribbean country, not an Italian city, and is a nonsensical distractor that would get an immediate laugh from any player.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Haiti' with an Italian city, e.g. 'Turin' variants are the answer — add 'Torino' is the answer itself — so use 'Trieste', 'Palermo', or 'Bari' instead.

issues.description: 'Puerto Rico' appears as a distractor in a question about which Italian city was Italy's first capital. Puerto Rico is a Caribbean US territory, not an Italian city — absurd as a distractor.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Puerto Rico' with an Italian city, e.g. 'Palermo', 'Bari', or 'Trieste'.

issues.description: 'Lviv' appears as a distractor in a question about which Caribbean island group is French overseas territory. Lviv is a Ukrainian city — completely off-category and would be immediately dismissible by any player.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Lviv' with a Caribbean island group, e.g. 'Saint Martin', 'Turks and Caicos', or 'Aruba'.

issues.description: 'Dnipro' appears twice in the distractor list (positions 5 and 8). Duplicate distractors are a data quality error.

issues.description: The question asks which three Scandinavian countries never had serfdom, with answer 'Finland, Norway, Sweden'. However, Finland was not historically considered a Scandinavian country — it is Nordic but not Scandinavian (Scandinavia traditionally refers to Norway, Sweden, and Denmark). Also Denmark DID have serfdom (stavnsbånd), which is acknowledged in the explanation, yet Denmark is listed in several distractors as if it might be a valid answer. More precisely: the question's framing of 'three Scandinavian countries' is inaccurate since Finland is Nordic, not Scandinavian.
issues.suggested_fix: Reword the question to say 'three Nordic countries' instead of 'three Scandinavian countries' to be accurate. Alternatively, keep Scandinavian and replace Finland with Iceland (which also lacked feudal serfdom, though it had vistarband).

issues.description: The claim that Salvador's Carnival holds the Guinness World Record for the world's biggest street party is contested. issues.description: The distractor 'Knights Hospitaller' is actually also a real medieval crusading military order that still exists today as the Sovereign Military Order of Malta. issues.description: 'Marienburg Castle' is listed as a distractor, but Marienburg IS the German name for Malbork Castle — they are the same place. The explanation even states 'Malbork (known in German as Marienburg)'. A distractor that is literally another name for the correct answer is invalid.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove 'Marienburg Castle' from the distractors and replace with a different castle, e.g. 'Vilnius Castle' or 'Wawel Castle'.

issues.description: The question 'The Cayman Islands has more registered companies than what?' is awkwardly phrased and reads like an incomplete sentence rather than a clean quiz question. It's a fill-in-the-blank format that doesn't work well for a multiple-choice card.

issues.description: The question 'Which Brazilian city received major European immigration waves between 1850 and 1950?' is too broad — São Paulo, Porto Alegre, Florianópolis, and Rio de Janeiro all received major European immigration in this period. The question is not uniquely answerable as Curitiba without additional context. São Paulo especially received enormous waves of Italian, German, and Japanese immigrants.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a specific qualifier to make the answer unambiguously Curitiba, e.g. 'Which Brazilian state capital is most known for its Polish and Ukrainian immigrant heritage from the 1850–1950 period?' or use the Paraná angle.

issues.description: Saying Urban II 'established the modern Roman Curia' slightly overstates the case. Urban II reorganized and formalized the Curia, but the Curia existed before him. The Wikipedia source says he 'set up the modern-day Roman Curia in the manner of a royal ecclesiastical court' — he reformed it rather than created it from nothing. The word 'established' in the question is misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Change question to 'Which medieval pope reorganized the Roman Curia into its modern form?' or 'Which pope formalized the Roman Curia as a royal-style ecclesiastical court?'

issues.description: The question calls Timur Babur's 'great-great-great-grandfather' (5 generations), but the genealogy is: Timur → Miran Shah → Abu Sa'id → Umar Shaikh → Babur — only 4 generations, making Timur Babur's great-great-grandfather, not great-great-great-grandfather.

issues.description: The question states Guevara helped precipitate the Cuban Missile Crisis 'by bringing Soviet missiles to Cuba.' This misattributes primary agency and could teach a false causal chain.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase question to focus on Guevara's actual role: e.g. 'What near-catastrophic Cold War event was Guevara a participant in, having helped defend the missile sites?' Or change the angle entirely to ask about the Bay of Pigs, where Guevara's role was clearer.

issues.description: The explanation states 'US combat forces from 1965 to 1973' but the war's accepted dates are 1955–1975. Using 1965–1973 understates US involvement and creates inconsistency with the claim about it being the 'longest US war at the time.' The US was militarily involved (advisors, funding, air power) from 1955, not just 1965.
issues.suggested_fix: Update explanation to note US involvement began with advisors in 1955, escalated to direct combat in 1965, and ended in 1975 — spanning 20 years. Alternatively, qualify the question to specify 'direct combat involvement.'

issues.description: The answer attributes bringing Soviet nuclear missiles to Cuba primarily to Che Guevara, which is misleading. The decision was made by Fidel Castro and Nikita Khrushchev. Guevara's personal role in initiating or brokering the missile deployment is not established in the historical record. Fidel Castro — listed as a distractor — was far more central to this decision. This misattribution could actively teach players incorrect history.
issues.suggested_fix: Change the correct answer to 'Fidel Castro' and rephrase the question accordingly, or reframe to focus on a role Guevara actually played (e.g., leading the militia that repelled the Bay of Pigs). The current framing is unsupported.

issues.description: The distractor 'They were brothers-in-law by marriage' is essentially the same as the correct answer 'Kamenev married Trotsky's sister' — both describe a brother-in-law relationship. A player who knows the correct answer could also select the distractor, or vice versa. This creates ambiguity.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the distractor 'They were brothers-in-law by marriage' and replace with something genuinely distinct, e.g., 'Kamenev was Trotsky's uncle' or 'They married sisters of each other.'

issues.description: This fact and 'history-9-11-death-toll' cover the exact same event with conflicting answers: 'About {3000}' vs. '{2,977}'. The Wikipedia figure is 2,977 victims. 'About 3000' is a rounded approximation that contradicts the more precise fact in the same batch. Having two facts about 9/11 deaths with different numbers will confuse players.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one of the two 9/11 death toll facts. If keeping this one, update to '{2,977}' for consistency. If keeping this one for its broader context (war on terror), ensure the two facts ask genuinely different questions, not just the same number worded differently.

issues.description: The distractor 'Iron was stronger' is historically partially true — iron (especially when worked into steel) is stronger than bronze, and this WAS one reason for the transition. Listing a true statement as a wrong answer is misleading and unfair to players who know their metallurgy. The intended correct answer ('Iron was more abundant') is also true but is not the only reason.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace the distractor 'Iron was stronger' with something clearly false, e.g., 'Iron glowed more brightly' or 'Bronze required water to smelt.' Alternatively, rephrase the question to specifically ask about iron's advantage in *availability/accessibility*, making 'more abundant' unambiguously the target answer.

issues.description: Categorized as 'ancient_classical' but Puyi's abdication in 1912 is squarely in the modern era. The Qing Dynasty (1644–1912) is not ancient or classical by any historical framework.
issues.suggested_fix: Change category_l2 from 'ancient_classical' to 'modern_contemporary'.

issues.description: Categorized as 'medieval' but the Dzungar Khanate (1634–1758) existed entirely in the early modern period — it was destroyed by the Qing dynasty in the mid-18th century, nearly 300 years after the conventional end of the medieval era.
issues.suggested_fix: Change category_l2 from 'medieval' to 'early_modern' or 'modern_contemporary'.

issues.description: The explanation states Luxemburg coined the phrase 'socialism or barbarism,' but this phrase originates with Friedrich Engels (from his 1880 work 'Socialism: Utopian and Scientific'). Luxemburg adapted and popularized it in her Junius Pamphlet but did not coin it. The question asks what she 'popularized,' which is accurate — but the explanation's implicit claim of authorship is misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Update the explanation to note the phrase was originally from Engels and was adapted/popularized by Luxemburg in her Junius Pamphlet. E.g., '...she powerfully adapted Engels's stark formulation: socialism or barbarism.'

issues.description: This fact (id ending in 'fastest-modernization', no number suffix) is a near-duplicate of 'history-empire-japan-fastest-modernization'. Both ask which country had the fastest modernization in history and both answer 'Japan'. The questions are worded slightly differently ('which country is considered to have undergone' vs. 'which country's post-1868 industrialization is often called') but test identical knowledge. Players will see essentially the same card twice.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one of the two facts. If both are kept, reframe one to ask something distinct, e.g., the specific era/reform name (Meiji Restoration) or a concrete outcome (e.g., how long it took Japan to become a recognized great power).

issues.description: Near-duplicate of 'history-bismarck-three-wars-unification'. Both ask how many wars Bismarck fought/provoked to unify Germany and both answer '3 wars' / 'Three wars'. The questions differ only in wording ('fight' vs. 'provoke') and answer format (numeral vs. word).

These test different specifics so they may be worth keeping, but together with the Latin alphabet duplicates and other Atatürk duplication, the coverage density on Atatürk is very high in one batch.
issues.suggested_fix: Keep both if the year-based fact is needed for spaced repetition variety. Otherwise remove the multiple-choice one (history-atatürk-abolished-caliphate-1924) since the year fact is more precise.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of 'history-francisco-pizarro-ransom'. Both cover Atahualpa's capture, ransom, and execution by Pizarro. These test different angles so they are more defensible as separate facts, but they share essentially identical explanations and context.

No content issue, just potential player confusion from similar IDs and shared explanations.
issues.suggested_fix: No change needed to content. Consider renaming IDs to better reflect what each question actually tests (e.g., 'history-beatles-formed-liverpool' and 'history-beatles-dissolved-1970').

issues.description: The 35-year calculation is off. Saddam joined the Ba'ath Party in 1957, but 1957 to 2003 is 46 years, not 35. The more defensible framing is his time in power: Ba'ath Party came to power in 1968, and 1968–2003 = 35 years. But the question says 'joined the Ba'ath Party in 1957' and then claims 35 years — that arithmetic is wrong inside the explanation itself.
issues.suggested_fix: Change explanation to: 'Saddam Hussein dominated Iraqi politics for 35 years from the Ba'ath Party's rise to power in 1968 to his overthrow in 2003.' Remove the reference to joining in 1957 as the start date.

issues.description: Descartes' 'Cogito, ergo sum' appears in his Meditations on First Philosophy (1641) and the Discourse on the Method (1637), but calling the 1637 Discourse the event that 'marked the start of the Enlightenment' is a stretch — most historians date the Enlightenment to the late 17th or early 18th century, not 1637. Additionally, 'Cogito, ergo sum' is actually Latin for 'I think, therefore I am,' but Descartes originally wrote it in French in the Discourse ('je pense, donc je suis'). The pure Latin form appears in the Meditations (1641), not 1637.
issues.suggested_fix: Revise question to not pin the Enlightenment's start to 1637. Better phrasing: 'Which famous Latin phrase encapsulates Descartes' foundational claim that the thinking self is the basis of all knowledge?' Or correct the year to 1641 and reference the Meditations.

issues.description: The correct answer 'A limp' is accurate, but the question asks what 'physical condition gave Timur the nickname.' A limp is a symptom/gait, not the underlying condition. The condition was a leg injury (possibly from an arrow). This is defensible but slightly imprecise — 'a limp' answers 'what was the disability' more than 'what condition.'
issues.suggested_fix: Minor: change question to 'What physical disability gave Timur the nickname Tamerlane, meaning the lame?' and answer to 'A lame leg' or keep as-is since 'limp' is the Wikidata value.

issues.description: Two distractors are very similar and redundant: 'Health collapse' and 'Vision blindness' — the latter also partially overlaps with the correct answer (the mystical vision left him unable to write, which contemporaries sometimes described as a breakdown). More importantly, 'Banned by Pope' and 'Church censure' are nearly identical distractors.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Vision blindness' and one of the near-duplicate church-related distractors with more distinct options, e.g. 'Completed the work' and 'Appointed Archbishop.'

issues.description: The explanation says Cortés 'scuttled (or burned) most of his fleet' — this is accurate hedging. However the correct_answer confidently says 'Burned his ships.' Historians generally agree he scuttled (sank) the ships rather than burned them. 'Burned his ships' is the popular myth; the more accurate term is 'scuttled.' The answer perpetuates a well-known historical inaccuracy.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'Scuttled (sank) his ships' and update the explanation to clearly state the ships were scuttled, not burned, while noting the common myth.

issues.description: The correct answer says 'About 10%' but the explanation states France covered ~12 million km². Earth's total land area is ~149 million km², making 12 million km² about 8%, not 10%. The answer and explanation are inconsistent, and neither is precisely right — 8% is closer to accurate.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'About {8}%' (numerical brace format) with no distractors, or keep as a distractor-based question and correct the answer to 'About one-twelfth' to match the actual ~8% figure.

issues.description: The question asks about 'Timbuktu's university' but the explanation names Sankore University specifically. The question conflates Timbuktu's scholarly institutions — the city had several mosques and madrasas that collectively constituted the scholarly infrastructure, not a single formal university. The 25,000 students figure is sourced from Wikipedia but refers to the entire scholarly community across the city's institutions, not one university.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase question to: 'How many students studied at Timbuktu's Islamic scholarly institutions at their medieval peak?' This is more accurate than implying a single university.

issues.description: The correct answer 'Up to one-third' and the explanation are internally inconsistent. The explanation states the population fell from 21 million to 13 million — a loss of 8 million out of 21 million, which is approximately 38%, closer to two-fifths than one-third. Saying 'up to one-third' when the explanation cites 38% in 'some regions' creates a mismatch.
issues.suggested_fix: Either change the correct answer to 'Up to two-fifths' to match the explanation's own figures, or revise the explanation to cite a one-third figure consistently.

issues.description: The correct answer 'Accidental choking' conflicts with the most widely cited account of Attila's death. Wikipedia and most historical sources state he died from a severe nosebleed (internal hemorrhage) on his wedding night — likely esophageal varices from heavy drinking — not choking on food. The Wikidata 'choking' classification appears to be a misclassification; a nosebleed/hemorrhage is not the same as choking. The question title even says 'death-by-nosebleed' in the ID, contradicting the answer.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'A severe nosebleed (hemorrhage)' and distractors accordingly. The fact ID itself ('history-attila-death-by-nosebleed') reveals what the correct answer should be. Update explanation to reflect the hemorrhage account.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which famous co-inventor of calculus studied under Spinoza?' — but Leibniz did not formally 'study under' Spinoza in any academic sense. issues.suggested_fix: Change question to 'Which famous co-inventor of calculus visited and studied the ideas of Spinoza?' or 'Which co-inventor of calculus was influenced by Spinoza and listed as his student in historical records?'

The $700 billion figure for Chernobyl is one estimate but calling it definitively 'the most expensive disaster in history' is an overstatement that many sources contest.
issues.suggested_fix: Change explanation to 'one of the most expensive disasters in history' rather than definitively 'the most expensive.' The question itself is fine; just soften the superlative in the explanation.

The distractors include 'His fist' — but if accounts genuinely vary and his fist is equally well-sourced, then 'His fist' could also be considered correct, making this a flawed question.

issues.description: The correct answer is '{14}' books traditionally attributed to Paul. The explanation then immediately says 'scholars only accept 7 as definitely authentic.' issues.description: The correct_answer 'Hyperfocus' is wrong for what the question describes. The question asks about the symptom where a person 'continues an action they intend to stop.' That is perseveration (inhibitory deficit), not hyperfocus. The explanation even says 'perseveration of actions despite intending to stop — known colloquially as hyperfocus,' which is an incorrect conflation. Hyperfocus is prolonged engagement on a desired task, not inability to stop an unwanted action.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'Perseveration' and rewrite the explanation to distinguish perseveration (inhibitory deficit causing inability to stop an ongoing action) from hyperfocus (prolonged, intense focus on a chosen activity). These are separate phenomena.

The answer format sounds like a comparative claim but reads oddly when selected as the quiz answer. issues.description: The question states 'How many cardinal signs of inflammation were identified by ancient physicians?' implying all five were ancient. However, only four signs (calor, dolor, rubor, tumor) were described by ancient Roman physician Celsus (1st century AD). The fifth sign, functio laesa (loss of function), was added by Rudolf Virchow in the 19th century — not by ancient physicians. Attributing all five to ancient physicians is historically inaccurate.
issues.suggested_fix: Either change the question to remove 'ancient physicians' — e.g., 'How many cardinal signs of inflammation are recognized in medicine?' — or acknowledge in the explanation that Virchow added the fifth sign in the 19th century. The answer of 'Five' remains correct; only the framing attributing all five to antiquity is wrong.

issues.description: Uranus was not imprisoned in Tartarus. In Hesiod's Theogony, Cronus castrated Uranus and separated him from Gaia, ending his reign — he was not imprisoned anywhere. It is the Titans (after the Titanomachy) and later the Giants who were imprisoned in Tartarus. The Wikidata claim cited appears to be erroneous or misapplied.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove this fact or reframe it. A corrected version could ask: 'What did Cronus do to his father Uranus to overthrow him?' with answer 'Castrated him with a sickle.' Alternatively, ask what Cronus did with the Titans after the Golden Age.

issues.description: 'Tellus' is listed as a distractor, but Tellus (also called Tellus Mater) is itself a valid Roman equivalent of Gaia — often considered synonymous with or indistinguishable from Terra in Roman religion. A player who knows this will correctly identify Tellus as also being right, making the question unfair.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Tellus' as a distractor with a clearly wrong option such as 'Juno' or 'Diana'. Optionally, acknowledge in the explanation that Terra and Tellus are closely related Roman earth goddesses.

issues.description: 'Scylla' is listed as a distractor for the question 'Which Greek sea monster was paired with Scylla in the Strait of Messina?' This is circular and confusing — the question names Scylla and then lists her as a wrong answer. A player who reads carefully may be thrown by seeing the subject of the question in the distractor list.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove 'Scylla' from the distractors and replace with a more sensible wrong answer, e.g., 'Hydra' or 'Ceto'.

issues.description: The question asks which entity 'spawned both Night and Earth as children,' but in Hesiod's Theogony, Gaia (Earth) did not arise as a child of Chaos — she arose spontaneously alongside Chaos as a co-primordial. Only Erebus and Nyx (Night) arose from Chaos. The question conflates two separate cosmogonic events. The explanation is also internally inconsistent, listing Uranus (Sky) as a child of Chaos, which contradicts standard Hesiodic cosmogony where Uranus was born of Gaia.
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase to ask only about Night/Darkness: 'In Hesiod's Theogony, which primordial void first gave rise to Darkness (Erebus) and Night (Nyx)?' with answer 'Chaos'. Remove the claim that Gaia was a child of Chaos from both the question and explanation.

issues.description: The explanation says 'Each dawn was conceived as Huitzilopochtli's victory over the forces of night' but the correct answer says he battled 'the night sky.' The answer oversimplifies.

issues.description: The answer 'A grotesque statue' is technically imprecise. In architectural terminology, a gargoyle specifically has a water drainage function (from Old French 'gargouille', throat). Grotesque figures without drainage spouts are technically 'grotesques' not gargoyles. The explanation acknowledges this ('functional waterspouts') but the answer itself is imprecise by calling it merely 'a grotesque statue.'
issues.suggested_fix: Change answer to 'A carved stone waterspout figure' or rephrase the question to 'What practical function did gargoyles originally serve on Gothic cathedrals?' with answer 'Draining rainwater away from walls.'

issues.description: The correct_answer says 'Saint Barbara and Saint Jerome' (both), but the explanation says 'syncretized with either Saint Barbara or Saint Jerome' (one or the other depending on tradition). These directly contradict each other. The 'either/or' framing in the explanation is the accurate one — different diaspora traditions use different saints.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to 'Saint Barbara or Saint Jerome' and reword the question to 'Which two Catholic saints is Shango most commonly syncretized with across different New World traditions?' OR keep the either/or and change the question to 'With which Catholic saint is Shango most commonly syncretized in Santería?' (answer: Saint Barbara).

issues.description: Inanna is listed as a distractor, but Ishtar IS Inanna — they are the exact same deity (Ishtar is the Akkadian/Babylonian name; Inanna is the Sumerian name). A knowledgeable player would correctly pick Inanna as a valid answer, making the question unanswerable without specialist knowledge of which naming convention the question uses. This is a factually wrong distractor.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove Inanna from the distractors and replace with another Mesopotamian goddess, e.g. 'Ninsun' or 'Gula'. The question could also be made clearer by specifying 'By her Akkadian name, which goddess...' to prevent ambiguity.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of myth-medb-irish-warrior-queen. issues.suggested_fix: Remove one. The 'why' version (myth-medb-irish-warrior-queen) is more interesting and informative than the 'what' version. Keep that one, remove this duplicate.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of myth-tristan-iseult-love-potion. Both questions ask what caused Tristan and Iseult to fall in love. (answer: 'A love potion'). Essentially the same question with a slightly simplified answer.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of myth-ptah-creator-god-craftsmen. These are distinct enough questions to both exist, but they share very similar explanations and may confuse players who see both.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of myth-sobek-crocodile-nile-power. issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural_sciences-outer-space-background-temperature. Both ask about the 2.7 Kelvin baseline temperature of outer space set by the cosmic microwave background radiation. The questions are nearly identically phrased.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural_sciences-mass-vs-weight. Both ask why an object weighs less on the Moon but has the same mass. The questions are phrased almost identically and test exactly the same fact.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one. Keep natural_sciences-mass-vs-weight which has a cleaner answer ('Lower gravity, same matter') and is categorized more precisely.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural_sciences-david-hilbert-cantor-set-theory. Both ask about Hilbert defending Cantor's controversial theory of infinite sets/set theory. The questions are close in wording and test the same knowledge.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural_sciences-enrico-fermi-statistical-mechanics. issues.description: Categorized under mythology_folklore / norse_celtic. Queen Medb is a figure of Irish mythology, which is Celtic — so 'norse_celtic' is technically inclusive, but the pairing of Norse and Celtic into one subcategory while this fact is purely Irish/Celtic (no Norse elements) may mislead players or create odd groupings. The companion fact myth-medb-irish-warrior-queen is under folk_legends, creating inconsistent categorization for the same subject.
issues.suggested_fix: Standardize both Medb facts to the same subcategory. If norse_celtic is meant to cover all Celtic mythology, that is acceptable. If not, move to folk_legends to match the other Medb fact.

issues.description: Bismuth (atomic number 83) is listed as a distractor, but bismuth was the classic rival claim for 'heaviest stable element' until 2003 when it was proven very slightly radioactive. The explanation cites this fact itself (bismuth is a decay endpoint). Having bismuth as a wrong-answer option while the explanation implicitly acknowledges the bismuth debate is contradictory and may confuse knowledgeable players. Lead (82) is the traditional textbook answer but the framing should acknowledge that this is the historical/practical answer.
issues.suggested_fix: Either add a qualifier to the question ('Which element was long considered to have the highest atomic number of any stable element?') or remove bismuth from the distractors and replace with a more clearly wrong option like Antimony or Gold.

issues.description: The explanation contains garbled Wikidata jargon: 'uranium-238's half-life varies between 159,200 and 4.5 billion years for different isotopes.' The 159,200-year figure is actually the half-life of uranium-235 (not uranium-238 or a variant of it). This is a copy-paste Wikidata artifact that incorrectly attributes another isotope's half-life to uranium-238.
issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite explanation: 'Uranium-238 has a half-life of approximately 4.5 billion years — nearly matching Earth's own age — which is why it is used to date the oldest rocks and meteorites. Its much rarer cousin uranium-235 has a far shorter half-life of about 700 million years.'

issues.description: The explanation field contains only 'What is the atomic number of calcium.' — literally just the question repeated verbatim. This is completely non-educational and provides zero context or interesting information for the player.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a real explanation, e.g.: 'Calcium's atomic number 20 means each calcium atom has 20 protons. It sits in Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) and Period 4 of the periodic table. Its 20th-element position makes it one of the most important biologically active elements — the most abundant metal in the human body.'

issues.description: The correct answer states 'Latin — from Kalium, the Latin name for potassium.' One distractor reads 'Arabic — from al-Qali, meaning alkali.' The explanation itself confirms that Kalium derives from the Arabic word al-qali. This makes the Arabic distractor not clearly wrong — Kalium IS ultimately Arabic-derived. The distinction between 'comes from Latin' vs 'comes from Arabic' is blurred enough that a knowledgeable player could reasonably select either. The distractor 'German — from Kalium, the German chemical name' is also very close to true since Kalium is also used in German.
issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite the correct answer to be more precise: 'New Latin (Neo-Latin) — Kalium was the chemical Latin name assigned to the element, ultimately traced to Arabic al-qali.' Remove the Arabic distractor and the German distractor to avoid near-correct options.

issues.description: The question is grammatically broken and incomplete: 'Francium holds the distinction of being the last element discovered in nature rather than how?' This is an incomplete sentence fragment — 'rather than how?' makes no grammatical sense as a standalone question. It appears to be a malformed fill-in-the-blank converted to a question.
issues.suggested_fix: Rewrite as: 'Francium was the last element to be discovered in nature. How were all subsequently discovered elements produced?' with correct answer 'By artificial synthesis (in nuclear reactors or accelerators).' Alternatively: 'What makes francium historically unique among elements discovered before 1940?' with answer 'It was the last element discovered occurring naturally, rather than being synthesized artificially.'

issues.description: The question asks 'What fundamental equation did Erwin Schrödinger develop in 1926 that describes how quantum systems evolve over time?' and the correct answer is 'The Schrödinger equation.' This is entirely circular — the answer is simply Schrödinger's name attached to 'equation.' A player with zero physics knowledge can guess this correctly just from the question containing Schrödinger's name. It tests almost nothing.
issues.suggested_fix: Reframe to test actual knowledge: 'What does the Schrödinger equation mathematically describe?' with answer 'How the quantum state (wave function) of a physical system changes over time.' Or ask about the year, or what field it founded, or what problem it solved.

issues.description: This fact is a near-duplicate of natural_sciences-cobalt-named-goblin (id appears ~13 facts later). Both ask about cobalt being named after a German word for goblin. The questions are phrased differently ('Which element is named after a German word for goblin because miners thought spirits had cursed their ore?' vs 'Which element's name comes from a German word for goblin, blamed for poisoning miners?') but cover identical content with the same answer and nearly identical explanations. In a deck of cards, a player will encounter both.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove one of the two cobalt-goblin facts. Keep the one with the richer explanation (natural_sciences-cobalt-named-goblin-miners has the more detailed 1735/Georg Brandt context). Replace the removed fact with a different cobalt fact (e.g., cobalt's role in lithium-ion batteries or its blue pigment use in art history).

issues.description: Duplicate of natural_sciences-cobalt-named-goblin-miners — see that entry. Both facts cover identical content: cobalt named after German 'goblin/kobold.'
issues.suggested_fix: Remove this fact or replace with distinct cobalt content.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural_sciences-nickel-mischievous-sprite. The questions differ slightly ('Which metal is named after a mischievous sprite because miners were tricked by ore that looked like copper?' vs 'Why did German miners call nickel ore Kupfernickel — Old Nick's copper?') but cover the same fact with the same answer.

Replace the generic one with a different nickel fact (e.g., nickel's role in stainless steel or its use in coins).

While the questions are phrased differently, all four ultimately test the same single fact. Three of the four should be replaced with genuinely different Röntgen content.
issues.suggested_fix: Keep one Röntgen Nobel Prize fact (the best-phrased one). Replace the others with distinct Röntgen facts, e.g.: the X-ray's medical impact, the naming of X-rays, or Röntgen donating his Nobel Prize money.

issues.description: Duplicate Nobel Prize fact — see natural_sciences-roentgen-first-nobel-physics entry. This is one of four facts covering Röntgen's first-ever Nobel Prize in Physics.

issues.description: Two issues: (1) This is a duplicate of natural_sciences-roentgen-first-nobel-physics — same core fact about Röntgen winning the first Nobel Prize in Physics. (2) The correct answer is spelled 'Wilhelm Rontgen' (missing umlaut) while the identical fact in other IDs consistently spells it 'Wilhelm Röntgen.' Inconsistent spelling of a proper name in the answer field.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove this duplicate. If kept, fix spelling to 'Wilhelm Röntgen.'

issues.description: The claim that pearl is 'the only gemstone produced by a living organism' is factually contestable. Amber (tree resin), coral (coral polyps), and jet (fossilized wood/organic matter) are all commonly listed as organic gemstones and all derive from living organisms. The distractors include Amber and Coral — both of which could be argued to also be gemstones from living organisms. While 'pearl' is the standard pub-quiz answer for this, listing amber and coral as clearly-wrong distractors is misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a qualifier: 'What is the only gemstone produced directly by a living animal (rather than fossilized or mineralized organic material)?' This cleanly distinguishes pearl from amber/jet (fossilized) and makes coral the only real close competitor, which can be handled in the explanation.

issues.suggested_fix: Minor issue — the two questions are sufficiently different in scope to coexist. No change strictly required, but consider whether having both is worth the card slot.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of natural-sciences-leeuwenhoek-draper-bacteria. Both ask about Leeuwenhoek's occupation before becoming the Father of Microbiology (draper/cloth merchant). The answers are the same ('Draper (cloth merchant)' vs 'Cloth merchant'). Essentially identical.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which of these did Leeuwenhoek NOT observe first through a microscope?' with only 3 distractors (Bacteria, Spermatozoa, Red blood cells) and the correct answer being 'Cell division.' The negative question format ('NOT observe') combined with very few answer options makes it feel like a trick question. Additionally, the explanation says he was 'among the first to see blood flow in capillaries' (not strictly first) but didn't mention cell division — the reasoning for why cell division is the odd one out is thin.
issues.suggested_fix: Consider rephrasing to a positive format, or ensure the explanation clearly explains who did first document cell division and why it definitively wasn't Leeuwenhoek.

issues.description: The question asks 'Which common chemical did Faraday discover as a chemist?' The answer 'Benzene' is correct, but the explanation also mentions he discovered carbon tetrachloride. A player reading the explanation would wonder why benzene was chosen over carbon tetrachloride as the answer. The question implies a unique discovery, but two are mentioned.
issues.suggested_fix: Either specify benzene in the question ('Faraday discovered which aromatic hydrocarbon in 1825?') or restructure the question to ask about both discoveries collectively, with the answer being a set rather than just benzene.

issues.description: The answer claims platinum is 'the only metal less reactive than gold' and the explanation calls gold 'second lowest in the reactivity series.' This is false. Multiple metals are less reactive than gold: palladium, iridium, osmium, rhodium, and ruthenium are all below gold on the electrochemical/reactivity series. Platinum is among the least reactive, but it is not the sole metal less reactive than gold.
issues.suggested_fix: Change correct_answer to something like 'Platinum group metals' or reframe the question to ask which specific metal is typically ranked just below gold in the standard reactivity series (where platinum is listed immediately below gold in many textbook versions). Alternatively, rewrite as: 'Gold resists almost all acids — what rare mixture of two acids is required to dissolve it?' and pivot to the aqua regia angle. If keeping this question, fix explanation: remove 'second lowest' and 'only platinum ranked as less reactive.'

issues.description: The distractor list contains 'Phosphorus' twice (positions 7 and 8). Phosphorus is not a metal anyway, so both entries are doubly wrong as distractors for 'most abundant metal in the human body.'
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the duplicate 'Phosphorus' entry. Replace with a valid metal distractor such as 'Zinc' or 'Potassium' (making sure not to duplicate other existing distractors).

issues.description: The last distractor is simply 'Copper' — clearly a data-entry error. The question asks for the name of the sound tin makes when bent. 'Copper' is the name of an element, not a sound name, and makes no sense as a distractor in this context.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Copper' with a plausible but wrong sound-name distractor, e.g. 'Tin groan', 'Metal whine', or 'Tin shriek'.

issues.description: The last distractor is 'Silver' — an element name, not a time period. The question asks 'How long ago was brass first used by humans?' so all distractors should be time durations (e.g. '1,000 years ago'). 'Silver' is clearly a copy-paste or generation error.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Silver' with a plausible wrong time period, e.g. '4,000 years ago' or '8,000 years ago'.

issues.description: The question answer is 'Less than {1} gram' but the explanation directly contradicts this: 'approximately 25 grams of astatine-210 exists in the Earth's crust.' These two statements cannot both be true. Wikipedia's figure is approximately 25 grams of astatine occurring naturally at any given time, not less than 1 gram.
issues.suggested_fix: Fix the correct_answer to '{25} grams' (or 'About 25 grams') to match both Wikipedia and the explanation. Or, if the intended answer is truly less than 1 gram (citing a different isotope breakdown), rewrite the explanation to match and remove the contradicting '25 grams' sentence.

issues.description: Near-duplicate of 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-cat' and 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-cat' (id). All three facts are about Schrödinger's cat thought experiment. 'sciences-schrodinger-cat' asks 'What famous thought experiment shows that a quantum particle can be in two states at once?' — 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-cat' asks 'What did Schrödinger's 1935 thought experiment use to illustrate quantum superposition?' — 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-entanglement-1935' asks 'What paradox-revealing thought experiment did Schrödinger devise in 1935?' All three answers converge on Schrödinger's cat. Three near-identical questions is excessive.
issues.suggested_fix: Consolidate to one Schrödinger's cat fact. The best is 'sciences-schrodinger-cat' (clearest framing). Delete 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-cat' and 'natural_sciences-schrodinger-entanglement-1935'.

issues.description: Duplicate of 'sciences-schrodinger-cat'. See that entry. The other version is more educational.
issues.suggested_fix: Delete this fact in favour of 'sciences-schrodinger-cat'.

issues.description: Third near-duplicate of the Schrödinger's cat theme. Question asks 'What paradox-revealing thought experiment did Schrödinger devise in 1935?' Answer is 'Schrödinger's cat'. Redundant given the other two facts.

issues.description: The correct_answer is formatted as '{2}–{5} cm per year' with two separate brace-wrapped numbers. The game's numerical answer system expects a single braced value. This dual-brace format may not render or score correctly.
issues.suggested_fix: Use a single representative value, e.g. '{2} to 5 cm per year' or '{5} cm per year (maximum typical speed)', or restructure as a non-numerical distractor question asking players to identify the correct range from options.

issues.description: States 'Over 10 million organic compounds are known.' The companion fact 'science-carbon-200m-compounds' in this same batch states 'about 200 million' carbon compounds have been described and indexed. These two facts directly contradict each other within the same batch. The 200 million figure (from CAS registry) is more current and accurate; the 10 million figure is significantly out of date.
issues.suggested_fix: Update the answer and explanation of 'sciences-organic-chemistry-carbon' to use the 200 million figure, aligning it with 'science-carbon-200m-compounds', or differentiate clearly (e.g., note that 10 million is a specific subset of synthesizable/known unique structures vs. 200 million indexed entries).

issues.description: Two distractors are self-contradictory nonsense: 'Edison's AC' (Edison promoted DC, not AC — this is internally incoherent) and 'Tesla's DC' (Tesla promoted AC, not DC — also internally incoherent). These aren't plausible wrong answers; they're confusing category errors that will mislead players.
issues.suggested_fix: Replace 'Edison's AC' with 'DC current (Westinghouse)' or just remove it. Replace 'Tesla's DC' with 'Tesla's wireless system' or another plausible alternative.

issues.description: The last distractor is 'Maxwell' — a bare name appearing after 'Maxwell's equations' is already listed as the first distractor. issues.suggested_fix: Remove the trailing 'Maxwell' distractor entirely. The list is already strong without it.

issues.description: The last distractor is 'Millikan' — a physicist's surname appearing in a list of actions Röntgen could have taken with his prize money. issues.suggested_fix: Remove 'Millikan' entirely. If a replacement is needed, add something like 'Spent it on his family' or 'Left it to charity upon death'.

issues.description: The correct answer is 'Mercury and Venus' but the explanation says 'engulfing Mercury and Venus and possibly Earth's orbit.' The explanation contradicts the answer by introducing uncertainty about Earth that isn't reflected in the answer. A player who reads the explanation will be confused about whether Earth is also correct.
issues.suggested_fix: Either update the explanation to say 'engulfing Mercury and Venus (Earth's fate is uncertain — its orbit may be reached but the planet itself may survive)' to match the answer, or change the correct answer to 'Mercury and Venus (possibly Earth)' to reflect the scientific nuance.

issues.description: The question asks which continent is NOT in Cupressus's native range, with 'Australia' as the correct answer. However, 'South America', 'Antarctica', and 'Arctic' (listed as distractors) are also not in Cupressus's native range. Multiple answers are technically correct, making the question ambiguous. 'Antarctica' in particular is a trivially obvious non-plant-habitat that telegraphs the puzzle.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove obviously non-plant-habitat options ('Antarctica', 'Arctic'). Rephrase to narrow down: 'Which of these continents does NOT have Cupressus as a native species?' with distractors being only continents where Cupressus IS native (Africa, Asia, North America, Europe). Alternatively, change to a positive framing: 'On which continent did Cupressus NOT naturally evolve?'

issues.description: The question states 'nine recognized dwarf planets' but the explanation says 'of these ten, two have been visited by spacecraft.' The question and explanation disagree on the total count of dwarf planets. The Wikipedia quote about 'ten' likely reflects a different counting convention (IAU recognizes 5 officially, informal lists go higher), creating an internal contradiction.
issues.suggested_fix: Align the explanation with the question's premise. Either update the question to say 'of the recognized dwarf planets' without specifying a number, or update the explanation to match the nine-count stated in the question.
